title: Rustic Farmhouse Round Coffee Table
summary: A round coffee table cut from joined 1x12 boards over an octagonal apron, on angled 2x4 legs tied by a three-piece triangle stretcher — the circle is cut AFTER the panel is joined and trimmed.
description:
This table builds its round top the reverse of how you might expect: join the 1x12 boards into a square panel first, sketch the circle on top, and only cut the actual circle after the octagonal apron is already glued to the underside — cutting the shape last means the apron and tabletop's edges land together in one pass rather than needing to be matched afterward. Keep pocket holes facing upward while doing this so a screw can be backed out if the circular saw's cut line happens to cross one.

The octagonal apron itself is built from angle-cut boards joined edge to edge into an eight-sided ring, sized to sit just inside the tabletop's circumference — building it as its own ring before attaching it to the tabletop underside is what keeps its shape true, rather than trying to piece it together directly under the round panel.

The base is a triangle-braced tripod: three 2x2 stretchers, each with a pocket hole on one end and a 30°-cut point on the other, joined at the center with trim screws, then attached to three 2x4 legs 6" up from the floor. **Balancing the frame while driving screws into this triangle-to-leg joint is a two-person job** — the source calls out that an extra set of hands genuinely helps here. Angling the trim screws in different directions as they're driven adds real resistance against the joint loosening, a small trick worth doing on every stretcher rather than just one.

tools.note: The octagon apron and triangle stretcher angle cuts.
Cutting the tabletop into a circle with a shop-made circle jig.

materials.name: 1x3, 8 ft
materials.note: Optional center support under the tabletop.

cutList.note: Joined into a panel, then cut into a 44" circle.
Both ends cut at 22-1/2° off square, ends not parallel.
One end cut at 10°, opposite end cut to a point at 30° off square.
Both ends cut at 10° off square, ends parallel, long point to short point.

steps.title: Build and shape the tabletop
steps.body: Lay out the 1x12 boards and roughly sketch a 44" diameter circle. Mark pocket hole locations inside the circle, drill 3/4" pocket holes, and join together with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws.

steps.title: Build the octagon apron
steps.body: Drill one 3/4" pocket hole on the inner side of each octagon board. Attach together to form an octagon ring.

steps.title: Attach the apron and cut the circle
steps.body: Place the octagon apron on top of the tabletop boards and attach with glue and 1-1/4" brad nails, avoiding areas the circular saw will cut through and keeping pocket holes facing upward. Cut the tabletop down into a circle using a shop-made circle-cutting jig.

steps.title: Build the triangle stretcher
steps.body: Cut the three 2x2 stretchers as specified, marking pocket holes and cuts first to avoid mistakes. Attach the three stretchers together at the center with glue and 2" trim screws, keeping all pocket holes oriented upward.

steps.title: Attach the stretcher to the legs
steps.body: Attach the triangle stretcher to the 2x4 legs with glue and 2-1/2" pocket hole screws, 6" up from the bottom of each leg. This step is easier with a second person to help balance the assembly.

steps.title: Add the leg cleats
steps.body: Set the base on a flat, level surface and confirm it sits evenly before the glue dries. Attach the leg cleats to the insides of the legs with 2" screws and glue, for attaching to the tabletop next.

steps.title: Attach the base to the top and finish
steps.body: Place the base on the underside of the tabletop and attach through the leg cleats with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws, two per joint. Optionally add the center support boards underneath for extra stiffness, attached with pocket holes. Sand, fill, and finish.

unresolvedImages.alt: Round farmhouse coffee table on a tripod triangle base
